before: This c. 1665 oil painting by Dutch Golden Age master Johannes Vermeer has been in the Mauritshuis in The Hague since 1902 and went by various names before acquiring its current title late in the 20th century.
after: Often called 'the Mona Lisa of the North,' this circa 1665 Vermeer painting shows a young woman in a blue-and-gold turban glancing over her shoulder against a dark background.

before: This High Renaissance sculptor, painter, architect, and poet was born in the Republic of Florence but spent most of his career in Rome.
after: He sculpted the marble 'David' and the 'Pietà,' and painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling.

before: Depicting a Midwestern farmer and his daughter before their Carpenter Gothic–style home in Eldon, Iowa, this 1930 Grant Wood oil painting has become one of the most widely parodied images in American art.
after: Depicting a stern, pitchfork-holding farmer and his daughter before their white, steep-gabled Iowa farmhouse, this 1930 Grant Wood oil painting is among the most widely parodied images in the nation's art.

before: Antoni Gaudí's unfinished Catholic basilica in Barcelona's Eixample district, begun in 1882 and still under construction, is the tallest church in the world and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
after: Begun in 1882 and still unfinished, this Antoni Gaudí basilica in Barcelona's Eixample district is the tallest church in the world and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

before: Norwegian artist Edvard Munch created four versions of this 1893 composition, each depicting a figure with an agonized face against a turbulent orange sky; Munch's diary records his feeling of 'an infinite scream passing through nature.'
after: Norwegian artist Edvard Munch created four versions of this 1893 composition, each depicting an anguished figure clutching its face against a turbulent orange sky.

before: During the 1687 siege of the Acropolis, Venetian artillery struck the building the Ottomans had converted into a gunpowder magazine, triggering an explosion that split the central colonnade and severely damaged a structure that had stood for over 2,000 years.
after: During a 1687 siege, Venetian artillery struck this building — which the occupying Ottomans had converted into a gunpowder magazine — triggering an explosion that split its central colonnade and severely damaged a structure that had already stood for over 2,000 years.

before: Completed in 1959 on Manhattan's Fifth Avenue after fifteen years of design and construction, Frank Lloyd Wright's bowl-shaped building — originally opened as the Museum of Non-Objective Painting — was initially controversial for its helical interior ramp.
after: Completed in 1959 on Manhattan's Fifth Avenue after fifteen years of design and construction, Frank Lloyd Wright's bowl-shaped building — first devoted to a collection of Non-Objective Painting — was initially controversial for its helical interior ramp.

before: This French artist gained notoriety between 1900 and 1905 as a leader of the movement whose French name means 'wild beasts,' known for intense, unnaturalistic colorism; during his final years, when illness prevented him from painting, he created major works in cut paper collage.
after: In his final years, when illness kept him from the easel, this French artist created some of his most celebrated works in cut-paper collage; decades earlier he had risen to fame among the Paris avant-garde for his radical, expressive use of color.

before: Praised by a French poet as 'the Apelles of our time' and celebrated for likenesses of Erasmus and Thomas More so definitive that it is through his work these Tudor-era figures are most vividly pictured today, this German-born Northern Renaissance painter is considered a great 'one-off' who founded no school.
after: Praised by a French poet as 'the Apelles of our time' and celebrated for likenesses of Erasmus and Thomas More so iconic that we still picture those Tudor-era figures largely as he painted them, this German-born Northern Renaissance painter is considered a great 'one-off' who founded no school.

before: The campanile, or freestanding bell tower, of its city's cathedral, this Italian structure is world-famous for a nearly four-degree tilt caused by the unstable ground on which its foundations were built.
after: This Italian structure is world-famous for its off-vertical angle of nearly four degrees, caused by the unstable ground on which its foundations were built.

before: Home to 1.5 million catalogued works across 17 curatorial departments, this encyclopedic New York City institution is the largest cultural repository in the Americas by floor area and the most-visited of its kind in the United States.
after: Home to more than 1.5 million catalogued works across 17 curatorial departments, this New York City institution is the largest of its kind in the Americas by floor area and the most-visited in the United States.

before: First recorded as an English color name in 1590, this shade of blue ranging from light azure to intense sky blue takes its name from the Latin caeruleus — itself probably derived from caelum, meaning 'heaven' or 'sky.'
after: First recorded as an English color name in 1590, this shade of blue — ranging from light azure to deep sky blue — takes its name from a Latin word meaning 'heaven' or 'sky.'

before: Commissioned between 1903 and 1907 by a Viennese Jewish sugar producer as a depiction of his wife, this oil-on-canvas work with gold leaf was seized by Nazi authorities in 1941 and displayed in an Austrian gallery for decades before its restitution to the original family's heirs.
after: Commissioned between 1903 and 1907 by a Viennese Jewish sugar magnate to honor his wife, this gilded oil-on-canvas work was seized by Nazi authorities in 1941 and held in an Austrian gallery for decades before its restitution to the original family's heirs.

before: When the U.S. capital relocated to its permanent home on the Potomac in 1800, this second president became the first to occupy the newly completed executive mansion.
after: When the U.S. capital settled on the Potomac in 1800, this president became the first to occupy the newly completed executive mansion.

before: This 1944 insurrection against Nazi occupation in the capital of Poland was followed by the systematic destruction of the city.
after: This 1944 insurrection against Nazi occupation was crushed within months, after which the city that staged it was systematically razed.

before: This 1980 trade union and civil resistance movement in a communist country is credited with triggering the fall of the Iron Curtain and pioneering the first peaceful transition from communist rule to liberal democracy.
after: This 1980 Polish trade union and civil-resistance movement is credited with triggering the fall of the Iron Curtain and pioneering the first peaceful transition from communist rule to liberal democracy.

before: This ruling house became the first to unify China in 221 BCE, opening two millennia of imperial governance across alternating periods of unity and division.
after: This dynasty became the first to unify China in 221 BCE, opening two millennia of imperial governance across alternating periods of unity and division.

before: Brokered in part by an Illinois senator later famous for debating Lincoln, this 1850 legislative deal sought to defuse sectional tensions over the expansion of slavery into newly acquired U.S. territories.
after: This 1850 legislative deal sought to defuse sectional tensions over the expansion of slavery into newly acquired U.S. territories.

before: Among Stanford University's first cohort of graduates, this American statesman organized wartime food relief for occupied Belgium during World War I, then directed the federal response to the catastrophic Mississippi River flooding of 1927, before his presidency was consumed by economic collapse.
after: Among Stanford University's first cohort of graduates, this American statesman organized wartime food relief for occupied Belgium during World War I, then directed the federal response to the catastrophic Mississippi River flooding of 1927.

before: A.J.P. Taylor hailed him as 'the only wholly admirable figure in modern history'; this general spent fourteen years in exile fighting with rebel forces in South America before returning to lead red-shirted volunteers in the campaign that forged a unified kingdom in southern Europe.
after: The historian A.J.P. Taylor hailed him as 'the only wholly admirable figure in modern history'; this general spent fourteen years in exile fighting with rebel forces in South America before returning to lead red-shirted volunteers in the campaign that forged a unified kingdom in southern Europe.

before: In 1917, watching the thrones of both of his first cousins fall — one the German Kaiser and the other the Russian Tsar — this British monarch quietly changed his dynasty's name away from its German-sounding ancestral designation.
after: In 1917, watching the thrones of both of his first cousins fall — one the German Kaiser and the other the Russian Tsar — this British monarch quietly changed his dynasty's name from its German-sounding ancestral designation to its current name, Windsor.

before: Described as the 'Father of His Country,' this Virginian Founding Father commanded the Continental Army to victory in the Revolutionary War before being sworn in as the nation's first president in 1789.
after: Described as the 'Father of His Country,' this General commanded the Continental Army to victory in the Revolutionary War before being sworn in as the nation's first president in 1789.

before: Appointed to the vice presidency under the 25th Amendment after Spiro Agnew's 1973 resignation, this Michigan Republican then assumed the presidency when Richard Nixon became the only president to resign from office — making him the sole person ever to hold both offices without winning a national election.
after: Prior to becoming president, this Republican was a center on the University of Michigan's national-championship football teams and was named the squad's most valuable player.

before: This island state — the only U.S. state that is an archipelago, the only one located south of the Tropic of Cancer, and one of only two states that were internationally recognized sovereign nations before joining the union — was admitted in 1959.
after: The last sovereign monarch of the Hawaiian Kingdom, this queen was deposed in an 1893 overthrow backed by American business interests; an accomplished musician, she composed the beloved farewell song 'Aloha ʻOe.'

before: Fought on June 18, 1815, in what is now Belgium, this final engagement of the Napoleonic era saw the French Imperial Army crushed by a British-led coalition and Prussian forces, ending its commander's last bid for European dominance.
after: Fought on June 18, 1815, in what is now Belgium, this final engagement of the Napoleonic era saw the French Imperial Army crushed by a British-led coalition and Prussian forces, ending Napoleon's last bid for power.

before: Chairman Mao Zedong launched this decade-long sociopolitical upheaval in China in May 1966 with the stated goal of preserving communism by purging remnants of capitalist and traditional elements from Chinese society; it lasted until his death.
after: Chairman Mao Zedong launched this decade-long sociopolitical upheaval in China in May 1966 with the stated goal of preserving communism by purging remnants of capitalist and traditional elements from Chinese society.

before: In 1781, the combined forces of George Washington, the Marquis de Lafayette, and the French Comte de Rochambeau hemmed in British General Cornwallis at a Virginia harbor town, forcing a surrender that effectively ended the American Revolutionary War.
after: This British general's surrender of roughly 8,000 troops at a Virginia harbor town in 1781 effectively ended the American Revolutionary War; he went on to serve as Governor-General of India and Lord Lieutenant of Ireland.

before: After the Mali Empire potentate Mansa Musa passed through around 1325, this ancient city north of the Niger River in West Africa rose from a seasonal trading post to a preeminent center of Islamic scholarship, with its Sankoré Madrasah drawing scholars from across the continent.
after: This 14th-century ruler of the Mali Empire, often cited as the wealthiest individual in recorded history, distributed so much gold during his pilgrimage to Mecca around 1324 that he reportedly depressed the metal's value across the regions he passed through.

before: Seized by Japanese forces in December 1941 — days after the attack on Pearl Harbor — this coral atoll in the Micronesian Pacific, composed of three islets, remained under occupation until Japan's 1945 surrender; the small flightless bird endemic to its shores went extinct during that occupation.
after: Seized by Japanese forces in December 1941, days after the attack on Pearl Harbor, this three-islet coral atoll in the Micronesian Pacific remained under occupation until Japan's 1945 surrender.

before: Finalized by the Continental Congress in November 1777 and ratified by all thirteen states by 1781, this document served as the new nation's first governing framework before being superseded by the Constitution.
after: Finalized by the Continental Congress in November 1777 and ratified by all thirteen states by 1781, this document served as the new American nation's first governing framework before being superseded by the Constitution.

before: This ancient Athenian democratic practice could banish any citizen for a decade, and was often deployed preemptively against someone the populace considered a threat to the state rather than as punishment for a past offense.
after: By an annual popular vote — each citizen scratching a name onto a pottery shard — Athenians could impose this ten-year banishment on someone deemed dangerous, with no charge or trial required.

before: Arising from the Council of Trent (1545–1563), this period of Catholic revival produced new religious orders, internal disciplinary changes, and doctrinal clarifications as a comprehensive response to the theological upheaval sweeping Protestant northern Europe.
after: This movement produced new religious orders, stricter internal discipline, and doctrinal clarifications as the Roman Church's comprehensive answer to the Protestant upheaval sweeping northern Europe.

before: This militia, formed in 1770 to defend the New Hampshire Grants territory from rival New York claims, was led by Ethan Allen and a man later infamous for his treason when it captured Fort Ticonderoga from the British in May 1775.
after: This militia, formed in 1770 to defend the New Hampshire Grants from rival New York claims, was led by Ethan Allen and Benedict Arnold when it captured Fort Ticonderoga from the British in May 1775.

before: This Homer epic follows the king of Ithaca — known in Latin as Ulysses — on his decade-long journey home from the Trojan War, while his wife Penelope fends off suitors vying for her hand.
after: This Homer epic recounts the ten-year homeward voyage of the king of Ithaca after the Trojan War.

before: Born when Halley's Comet appeared in 1835, this author of Tom Sawyer and Huckleberry Finn predicted he would die when the comet returned — and did so the day after its closest approach to the Sun in 1910.
after: This American author and humorist was born in 1835 as Halley’s Comet appeared and died the day after it returned in 1910 — a coincidence he had predicted.

before: Its famous opening line, 'Call me Ishmael,' is one of world literature's most quoted; this 1851 Herman Melville novel centers on the obsessive Captain Ahab's pursuit of a white sperm whale that severed his leg.
after: First published in London as 'The Whale' in October 1851, this novel was a commercial failure and out of print at its author's death in 1891; its standing as a Great American Novel was only established in the 20th century.

before: Written in 1818 by Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley, this Gothic novel — often considered an early example of science fiction — follows a scientist who creates a living creature from dead matter.
after: This 1818 Gothic novel — often considered an early example of science fiction — follows a scientist who creates a living creature from dead matter.

before: Published in 1850 and set in the Puritan Massachusetts Bay Colony of the 1640s, this Nathaniel Hawthorne novel follows Hester Prynne, who is forced to wear an embroidered letter 'A' as punishment for adultery.
after: Published in 1850 and set in the Puritan Massachusetts Bay Colony of the 1640s, this Nathaniel Hawthorne novel follows Hester Prynne, who is forced to wear an embroidered crimson ‘A’ on her clothing as punishment for adultery.

before: In this Italian narrative poem, a pilgrim travels through Hell and Purgatory guided by the Roman poet Virgil — who represents human reason — then continues into Paradise led by Beatrice, who represents divine revelation.
after: This tripartite 14th-century poem ends with its narrator led through heaven by his idealized beloved Beatrice and, finally, by Saint Bernard of Clairvaux.

before: This German literary term describes a coming-of-age narrative focused on the moral and psychological growth of a young protagonist; Charles Dickens's Great Expectations is often cited as one of its defining examples.
after: This German literary term describes a coming-of-age narrative focused on the moral and psychological growth of a young protagonist from youth into maturity.

before: Daniel Defoe's 1719 novel — often credited as the first English work of realistic fiction — follows a man born with the surname Kreutznaer, stranded on a tropical island near Venezuela for 28 years.
after: Daniel Defoe's 1719 novel — often credited as the first English work of realistic fiction — follows a man stranded on a tropical island near Venezuela for 28 years.

before: Though titled for a famous group of royal guards, this 1844 Dumas novel was composed with ghostwriter Auguste Maquet and centers on the young Gascon d'Artagnan, who longs to join their ranks.
after: Loosely inspired by a 1700 pseudo-memoir of a real Gascon soldier in Louis XIII's guard, this 1844 French serial novel pits its heroes against Cardinal Richelieu and the cunning Milady de Winter.

before: This Shakespeare villain's role, along with that of his general, constitutes two of the four longest parts Shakespeare ever wrote; critics have long debated what truly motivates his destructive scheming.
after: This Shakespeare villain is such a central part of the play that he has more lines than the story's main character and is one of the most verbose characters in all of Shakespeare's works.

before: Created by Edward Stratemeyer and ghostwritten under the collective pseudonym Carolyn Keene, this fictional teenage amateur sleuth has appeared in mystery book series, movies, and TV shows since 1930.
after: Conceived by book packager Edward Stratemeyer and written by a rotating team of ghostwriters all credited to the invented author 'Carolyn Keene,' this teenage amateur sleuth has starred in mystery books, films, and TV since 1930.

before: Oscar Wilde and Walter Pater were leading figures of this late 19th-century art movement, which held that literature and the arts should be judged by their beauty alone — not by moral or didactic purpose — a stance captured in the slogan 'art for art's sake.'
after: Oscar Wilde and Walter Pater were leading figures of this late 19th-century art movement, which held that literature and the arts should be judged by their beauty alone, not by any moral or didactic purpose.

before: First published in the January 1861 issue of The Atlantic Monthly and later retitled 'The Landlord's Tale' in an 1863 collection, this Henry Wadsworth Longfellow narrative poem commemorates — with significant historical inaccuracies — a patriot's midnight warning ride of April 1775.
after: First published in the January 1861 issue of The Atlantic Monthly and later retitled 'The Landlord's Tale' in an 1863 collection, this Henry Wadsworth Longfellow narrative poem commemorates — with significant historical inaccuracies — a patriot's midnight gallop of April 1775 to rouse the colonial militia.

before: A medieval German legend tells of a curiously dressed rat catcher who lured a town's vermin away with his flute — and, when the town refused to pay him, led its children away by the same means.
after: A medieval German legend tells of a curiously dressed rat catcher who lured a town's vermin away with his flute and then, when the town refused to pay him, led its children away by the same means.

before: This 1943 Betty Smith novel about a girl coming of age in a New York City tenement was so popular it went to a second printing before its official publication date and was later named by the Library of Congress as one of 'Books that Shaped America.'
after: This 1943 Betty Smith novel about a girl coming of age in a New York City tenement was so popular it went to a second printing before its official publication date and was later named by the Library of Congress as one of the 'Books That Shaped America.'

before: During her lifetime, this English author published her six major novels anonymously, with title pages identifying her only as 'By a Lady,' while her sharp social commentary and irony would eventually earn her a place among the most widely read writers in English literature.
after: All six major novels by this English author were originally published anonymously, their title pages identifying her only as 'By a Lady'; her sharp social commentary and irony eventually earned her a place among the most widely read writers in English literature.

before: In Charlotte Brontë's 1847 novel, this character is Rochester's first wife, locked in a third-floor room at Thornfield Hall whose existence is deliberately hidden from the heroine.
after: In Charlotte Brontë's 1847 novel, this madwoman — the first wife of Thornfield Hall's brooding master — is confined to a hidden third-floor room, her existence concealed from the young governess who has come to love him.

before: The pen name adopted by Mary Ann Evans, this Victorian author's last completed novel — published in eight installments in 1876 — follows both a spirited young woman named Gwendolen and a young man on the cusp of discovering his Jewish heritage.
after: This Victorian novelist's final work, published in 1876, interweaves the story of the spirited Gwendolen Harleth with that of a young man awakening to his Jewish heritage — an ambitious departure from the English provincial life her earlier novels charted.

before: This Tchaikovsky concert piece, written in 1880, commemorates Russia's successful defense against a French invasion; it was first performed in Moscow on August 20, 1882, under a tent near the still-unfinished Cathedral of Christ the Saviour.
after: This Tchaikovsky concert piece — written in 1880, sixty-eight years after the year that lends the work its name — commemorates Russia's successful defense against the invading French; it premiered in Moscow in 1882 near the still-unfinished Cathedral of Christ the Saviour.

before: The score for this 1998 Coen Brothers film, composed by Carter Burwell, underscores the story of a Los Angeles slacker who is mistaken for a wealthy man of the same surname; Joel Coen described it as his 'Chandler kind of story.'
after: This 1998 Coen Brothers film follows a Los Angeles slacker who is mistaken for a wealthy man of the same surname; Joel Coen described it as his 'Chandler kind of story.'

before: Along with The Nutcracker and The Sleeping Beauty, this completes the trio of Tchaikovsky's most celebrated ballet scores.
after: This Tchaikovsky ballet — drawn from a folk tale of a princess under a sorcerer’s curse — flopped at its 1877 Bolshoi premiere as “too Wagnerian,” yet the 1895 Petipa–Ivanov revival made it one of the world’s most-performed.

before: This American jazz saxophonist, bandleader, and composer is regarded as among the most influential and acclaimed figures in the history of jazz and 20th-century music.
after: Critic Ira Gitler coined the phrase “sheets of sound” for this musician’s dense, cascading style; his increasingly spiritual music culminated in the 1965 album “A Love Supreme,” and he received a special Pulitzer Prize in 2007, four decades after his death.

before: In music, this term names a formation of exactly seven performers — one larger than a sextet and one smaller than an octet — and can also apply to a seven-line stanza of poetry.
after: In music, this term names a formation of exactly seven performers, and can also apply to a seven-line stanza of poetry.

before: The B-side of this 1976 KC and the Sunshine Band chart-topper — their third number-one on both the Billboard Hot 100 and Hot Soul Singles — was "Boogie Shoes," which only became a hit after appearing on a disco film soundtrack the following year.
after: The B-side of this 1976 KC and the Sunshine Band chart-topper was "Boogie Shoes," which only became a hit after appearing on a disco film soundtrack the following year.

before: He won the Academy Award for Best Director for the musical drama Cabaret in 1972 and the Palme d'Or at Cannes for All That Jazz in 1979, making him arguably the most decorated figure in jazz dance of the 20th century.
after: This director and choreographer won the 1972 Academy Award for Best Director for the musical drama 'Cabaret' and the 1979 Palme d'Or at Cannes for 'All That Jazz.'

before: This Hungarian Romantic composer coined the concept of the symphonic poem, invented the modern piano masterclass as a teaching format, and generated such hysterical devotion during his 1830s European tours that a new word was coined from his name.
after: This Hungarian Romantic composer coined the concept of the symphonic poem, invented the modern piano masterclass as a teaching format, and generated such hysterical devotion during his 1840s European tours that a new word was coined from his name.

before: Revered as Finland's national composer, this man produced no major works in the last three decades of his life — a mysterious self-imposed halt his countrymen named after the lakeside town where he spent his final years.
after: Revered as Finland's national composer, this man produced almost no major works in the last three decades of his life — a prolonged, self-imposed silence that has puzzled music historians ever since.

before: Although Louis Armstrong's recording topped the pop chart in the United Kingdom in April 1968, it performed poorly in the United States because the ABC Records president disliked it and refused to promote it.
after: Although this Louis Armstrong recording topped the pop chart in the United Kingdom in April 1968, it performed poorly in the United States because the ABC Records president disliked it and refused to promote it.

before: This California-born singer collaborated with Francis Ford Coppola on a 1982 film score and was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2011, with Neil Young introducing him as 'a performer, singer, actor, magician, spirit guide, changeling.'
after: This California-born singer wrote the score for Francis Ford Coppola's 1982 film 'One from the Heart' and was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2011, with Neil Young introducing him as 'a performer, singer, actor, magician, spirit guide, changeling.'

before: In 1972 alone, this choreographer-director won the Tony Award (for 'Pippin'), the Academy Award for Best Director (for 'Cabaret'), and an Emmy (for 'Liza with a Z') — a remarkable sweep of major dramatic honors in a single year.
after: In 1958 this dancer founded a landmark American company to express the universality of the African-American experience; his choreographic masterwork 'Revelations' is recognized as one of the most popular and most performed ballets in the world.

before: Based on a Prosper Mérimée novella and first staged in Paris in March 1875, this opera shocked its opening-night audience with its unconventional realism; its French composer died after just 33 performances, never knowing the work would achieve international acclaim.
after: Based on a Prosper Mérimée novella and first staged in Paris in March 1875, this opera shocked its opening-night audience by putting a defiant, free-spirited heroine and an on-stage killing before the public; its French composer died after just 33 performances, never knowing the work would achieve international acclaim.

before: First released independently in December 2018, this rap single was re-signed to Columbia Records and remixed with country singer Billy Ray Cyrus, sparking widespread debate about its genre classification.
after: First released independently in late 2018 and later re-signed to Columbia Records, this genre-blurring debut single sparked widespread debate over whether it counted as country — a controversy only stoked when a remix added an established country-music star.

before: Recorded at Sigma Sound Studios in Philadelphia in 1974, this Bowie LP — its creator called its style 'plastic soul' — featured then-unknown backing vocalist Luther Vandross and yielded Bowie's first U.S. number-one single.
after: Recorded at Sigma Sound Studios in Philadelphia in 1974, this David Bowie LP — he called its style 'plastic soul' — featured then-unknown backing vocalist Luther Vandross and yielded Bowie's first U.S. number-one single.

before: Originally drawn from a T.S. Eliot poem in his 1939 'Old Possum's Book of Practical Cats,' this character in Andrew Lloyd Webber's long-running musical is renowned for a solo dance called the 'Conjuring Turn,' which culminates in 24 consecutive fouettés en tournant.
after: Originally drawn from a T.S. Eliot poem in his 1939 'Old Possum's Book of Practical Cats,' this character in Andrew Lloyd Webber's long-running musical is renowned for a solo dance, the 'Conjuring Turn,' that culminates in 24 consecutive fouettés — the rapid whipping turns of ballet.

before: At the 64th Academy Awards, this South Central Los Angeles coming-of-age film's writer-director became both the youngest person and the first African American nominated for Best Director.
after: At the 64th Academy Awards, John Singleton — the writer-director of this South Central Los Angeles coming-of-age film — became both the youngest person and the first African American nominated for Best Director.

before: This fictional surgeon, created by Shonda Rhimes and portrayed by Ellen Pompeo, is the titular protagonist of ABC's long-running medical drama series that premiered on March 27, 2005.
after: This fictional surgeon, created by Shonda Rhimes and portrayed by Ellen Pompeo, is the title character of an ABC medical drama that premiered in 2005 and became one of the longest-running scripted primetime series in American television.

before: This pale-skinned fictional character with black pigtails, created by cartoonist Charles Addams, is portrayed as morbid and fascinated by the macabre; Christina Ricci played her in 1991 and Jenna Ortega in a 2022 streaming series.
after: This pale-skinned fictional character with black pigtails, the macabre-loving daughter in a darkly comic family that originated as a 1930s New Yorker cartoon, was played by Christina Ricci in 1991 and Jenna Ortega in a 2022 streaming series.

before: This charismatic but enigmatic protagonist of AMC's Mad Men, portrayed by Jon Hamm, is a Manhattan advertising executive who assumed a dead soldier's name and identity; he was born Richard Whitman.
after: This charismatic but enigmatic protagonist, portrayed by Jon Hamm, is a Manhattan advertising executive who assumed a dead soldier's name and identity; he was born Richard Whitman.

before: The world's largest Volksfest, held annually in Munich on the Theresienwiese from mid-September to the first Sunday in October, draws around seven million visitors and centers on beer.
after: The world's largest folk celebration, held annually in Munich on the Theresienwiese for about two weeks from mid-September, it draws around seven million visitors and centers on beer.

before: Originating in Rhode Island, this New England appetizer is served on the halfshell topped with breadcrumbs and bacon, and is named for the type of gambling establishment associated with its invention.
after: Originating in Rhode Island, this New England appetizer is served on the halfshell topped with breadcrumbs and bacon, and is named for the type of establishment associated with its invention.

before: Released in 2022, this blockbuster sequel became the highest-grossing film of its star's career, clearing over $1.5 billion at the worldwide box office and receiving an Academy Award nomination for Best Picture.
after: Arriving 36 years after its 1986 original, this 2022 sequel about a daredevil Navy fighter pilot became the highest-grossing film of its star's career, clearing over $1.5 billion at the worldwide box office and receiving an Academy Award nomination for Best Picture.

before: In February 2026, the media conglomerate that operates an all-news cable network, a premium cable channel known for prestige programming, and a major streaming platform agreed to a $110.9 billion acquisition by a rival entertainment company.
after: In February 2026, this media conglomerate — the parent of HBO, CNN, and the Max streaming service — agreed to a $110.9 billion takeover by Paramount Skydance.

before: In this 2004 romantic drama directed by Nick Cassavetes, an elderly man reads aloud a handwritten love story to a fellow nursing-home resident — a framing device whose emotional weight is fully revealed only in the film's final moments.
after: In this 2004 romantic drama directed by Nick Cassavetes, an elderly man reads aloud a handwritten love story to a fellow nursing-home resident.

before: This ABC reality dating series, which premiered on September 28, 2023 as a spin-off featuring senior contestants rather than the usual twenty-somethings, returned for a second season in September 2025.
after: Premiering in 2023, this senior-citizen spin-off of a long-running ABC dating franchise put a retiree at the center of the rose ceremonies; it returned for a second season in 2025.

before: A short clip capturing a cast or crew member's on-set mistake, this term also covers on-air errors during live broadcasts — and has become the staple of viral compilation videos online.
after: A single outtake clip capturing a flubbed line or on-set mistake — the kind later strung together into the viral compilation videos that pad a film's end credits.

before: Credited by its creator — Wieden+Kennedy founder Dan Wieden — to the final words of a convicted murderer on death row, this three-word Nike slogan was coined in 1988.
after: Credited by its creator — Wieden+Kennedy founder Dan Wieden — to the final words of a convicted murderer on death row, this three-word slogan was coined in 1988.

before: The 1937 Hungarian stage play Parfumerie by Miklós László reached the screen for the fourth time in this 1998 romantic comedy set in the early days of internet communication.
after: Set in the early days of email, this 1998 romantic comedy was the fourth screen adaptation of the 1937 Hungarian stage play Parfumerie by Miklós László.

before: This Titan was chained to a rock and condemned to have his liver consumed by an eagle each day as punishment for bestowing fire upon humanity.
after: For slipping mortals a gift stolen from the gods of Olympus, this defiant Titan was bound to a crag where an eagle returned each day to feast on his ever-regrowing liver.

before: This Olympian twin of Artemis served as patron deity of Delphi and was the god through whom the most famous oracle of the ancient world spoke.
after: God of healing yet also of plague, of music and of the far-shooting bow, this deity was held up by the Greeks as the very ideal of youthful male beauty — the kouros made divine.

before: When the three sons of Cronus divided the cosmos by lot after defeating the Titans, this eldest brother drew the underworld as his domain.
after: After the three brothers who toppled the Titans split the cosmos by lot, this eldest of them drew neither the sky nor the sea, but the realm of the dead.

before: This god of the sea and earthquakes carried a trident and was believed to have created horses with a strike of it; his Roman counterpart is Neptune.
after: Bronze Age clay tablets at Pylos rank this 'Earth-Shaker' among the chief gods, and in the hills of remote Arcadia he was once worshipped in the form of a horse.

before: This Norse deity wielded the hammer Mjölnir and was venerated during the Viking Age as a protector of mankind and farmers against giants and chaos.
after: Swinging the hammer Mjölnir against the giants of chaos, this red-bearded god was the steadfast protector of mankind and of the Viking farmer.

before: This Norse shape-shifter once took the form of a mare, was impregnated by a stallion, and gave birth to the eight-legged horse Sleipnir.
after: By a giantess this trickster sired three bringers of doom — the wolf Fenrir, the world-serpent, and the death-queen Hel.

before: This chief of the Norse Æsir pantheon was accompanied by two ravens named Huginn and Muninn and two wolves, Geri and Freki, and fathered both Baldr and Thor.
after: Each dawn he loosed two ravens — Thought and Memory — to range across the nine worlds and murmur back at dusk all they had witnessed.

before: This major Egyptian goddess resurrected her slain husband and was considered the divine mother of the pharaoh, whose power was likened to her son Horus.
after: Egyptians held that this goddess's magic ushered souls safely into the afterlife, and that every reigning pharaoh was the living Horus she had borne and shielded.

before: This jackal-headed deity of ancient Egypt presided over the 'Weighing of the Heart' ceremony, in which a deceased soul's virtue was judged against a feather.
after: Protector of graves and lord of embalming, this canine-headed Egyptian god led the newly dead along the road to the underworld and its judgment.

before: This Egyptian god was murdered and dismembered by his own brother Set, then reassembled by his devoted wife to be resurrected and reign over the dead.
after: Shown as a mummy in a tall plumed crown, gripping a crook and flail, this lord of grain and the dead let Egyptians read the harvest's yearly withering and regrowth as his own.

before: This Athenian philosopher wrote nothing himself, yet his method of rigorous questioning gave rise to a whole literary genre of philosophical dialogues recorded by his student Plato.
after: Professing that he knew only that he knew nothing, this Athenian set down not a single word of his own — yet his needling question-and-answer cross-examination survives through the pens of his students.

before: This Enlightenment thinker from Königsberg argued in his *Critique of Pure Reason* that space and time are forms of intuition imposed by the human mind, not features of reality itself.
after: This Enlightenment philosopher staged a 'Copernican' turn in thought: space, time, and cause are not features of the world but forms the mind imposes — so the thing-in-itself stays forever beyond knowing.

before: Appointed a full professor at age 24, this German thinker spent his later years declaring religion's decline before suffering a complete mental collapse at 44 from which he never recovered.
after: A classical-philology prodigy made a Basel professor at just 24, this German later proclaimed that 'God is dead' and conjured the Übermensch and eternal recurrence before madness took him at 44.

before: This Indian religion, the world's fourth-largest, teaches that liberation from suffering comes through a Middle Way between extreme asceticism and sensory indulgence.
after: Arising some 2,500 years ago as a śramaṇa movement on the eastern Gangetic plain, this faith draws its name and its path from a teacher remembered as 'the Awakened One.'

before: In Indian religious traditions including Buddhism and Hinduism, this concept represents ultimate liberation — the extinguishing of personal desire and release from the cycle of rebirth known as saṃsāra.
after: In some eastern religious traditions, this concept represents ultimate liberation — the extinguishing of personal desire and release from the cycle of rebirth known as saṃsāra.

before: In Hesiod's Theogony, Zeus swallowed his pregnant consort Metis, and this goddess of wisdom sprang fully armored from his forehead; she was later identified with the Roman Minerva.
after: In Hesiod's Theogony, Zeus swallowed his pregnant consort Metis, and this goddess sprang fully armored from his forehead.

before: The cult of this Greek goddess associated with love and beauty may have derived from the Phoenician goddess Astarte, herself a cognate of the East Semitic Ishtar and the older Sumerian Inanna.
after: The cult of this Greek goddess may have derived from the Phoenician goddess Astarte, herself a cognate of the East Semitic Ishtar and the older Sumerian Inanna.

before: This Catholic resurgence — also called the Catholic Revival — arose in response to the Protestant challenge and was formally shaped by the Council of Trent's decrees issued between 1545 and 1563.
after: Convened in three sessions between 1545 and 1563 in a city of the Holy Roman Empire's Italian north, this Catholic ecumenical gathering codified doctrine on scripture and the sacraments and drove the Church's answer to the Protestant Reformation.

before: This first-century Jewish preacher, active near the Jordan River, announced a messianic figure greater than himself, performed a ritual immersion of Jesus, and was later beheaded at the order of Herod Antipas for denouncing the ruler's unlawful remarriage.
after: This first-century preacher at the Jordan River foretold a messiah greater than himself before being beheaded by Herod Antipas for condemning the ruler's unlawful remarriage.

before: Often called the father of modern philosophy, this 17th-century French mathematician coined the axiom 'I think, therefore I am' and fused algebra with geometry into analytic geometry — a field that still bears his name.
after: Often called the father of modern philosophy, this 17th-century French thinker fused algebra with geometry to pioneer the field of analytic geometry.

before: Alfred North Whitehead characterized the entire European philosophical tradition as 'a series of footnotes' to this Athenian's work; a student of Socrates and teacher of Aristotle, he founded the Academy and articulated the Theory of Forms.
after: Alfred North Whitehead characterized the entire European philosophical tradition as 'a series of footnotes' to this Athenian's work.

before: First published in 1830 by Joseph Smith, this sacred scripture describes an appearance of Jesus in the Americas after his resurrection and serves as the 'keystone' of the Latter Day Saint movement.
after: Said to be translated from golden plates delivered by the angel Moroni and inscribed in a script its prophets called 'reformed Egyptian,' this 1830 scripture chronicles the ancient American peoples known as the Nephites and Lamanites.

before: Found across world traditions — from the sacred pillar of wood in Genesis's Garden of Eden to the Norse Yggdrasil — this universal cosmic archetype represents the connection between the underworld, the earth, and the heavens as the axis of all creation.
after: From the Norse Yggdrasil to the garden at the center of Eden, this universal cosmic archetype links the underworld, the earth, and the heavens as the axis of all creation.

before: Described in Genesis as a 'mighty hunter before the Lord,' this great-grandson of Noah founded a kingdom in Shinar and is linked by tradition to the Tower of Babel; in modern slang, his name has come to mean a bumbling fool — a complete inversion of its original sense.
after: This priest-king of Salem brought out bread and wine to bless Abram in Genesis and received a tithe from him; appearing with no recorded lineage or death, he is later invoked in the Letter to the Hebrews as the eternal pattern for the priesthood of Christ.

before: Sired by the sea-god and born when a Greek hero slew the Gorgon — his blood mixing with sea-foam — this divine stallion stamped his hoof on Mount Helicon to create the Muses' sacred spring Hippocrene and later bore Zeus's thunderbolts to Olympus.
after: Astride a winged horse and armed with a lead-tipped spear, this Corinthian hero slew the fire-breathing Chimera; emboldened, he tried to ride up to the home of the gods, but a gadfly sent to sting his mount pitched him back to earth.

before: First appearing during the First Punic War as a Roman renaming of the Greek goddess Nike, this deified personification of triumph had — unlike her Greek counterpart — virtually no mythology of her own; her name is now far better known as a given name, a long-reigning British queen, and a Canadian provincial capital.
after: Romanized from the Greek Nike during the First Punic War, she came to symbolize Rome's hegemony and right to rule; a deified abstraction with virtually no mythology of her own, she left behind a name now borne by far more women than worshippers.

before: More than half the diameter of its companion, this body shares a center of mass that lies in the space between them rather than inside either — leading astronomers to classify the pair as a binary system.
after: The largest moon of Pluto, it is so large relative to its planet that the two orbit a balance point in the empty space between them.

before: Sexually dimorphic with females considerably larger than males, this large crow-sized raptor with a blue-grey back and distinctive black head was historically known as the 'duck hawk' in North America.
after: Reaching speeds over 320 km/h in its hunting stoop — the fastest of any animal on Earth — this crow-sized raptor with a blue-grey back and black head was historically known as the 'duck hawk' in North America.

before: This temporary physiological condition arises when a traveler crosses several time zones rapidly, leaving the body's internal clock out of sync with local time — as when a New York–to–London passenger finds it feels five hours earlier than it actually is.
after: This temporary physiological condition arises when a traveler crosses several time zones rapidly, leaving the body's internal clock out of sync with local time.

before: When two identical periodic waves traveling in opposite directions are superimposed, they produce this stationary interference pattern, which exhibits fixed positions of zero amplitude called nodes.
after: When two identical waves travel in opposite directions and superimpose — as on a plucked guitar string or inside an organ pipe — they form this stationary pattern, whose fixed points of zero motion, called nodes, set the resonant pitches a musical instrument can sound.

before: First domesticated in Central Asia around 4,000 BCE, these mammals — bred into more than 300 distinct varieties — can sleep both standing up and lying down; females carry their young for approximately eleven months.
after: First domesticated in Central Asia around 4,000 BCE, these mammals — bred into more than 300 distinct varieties — can sleep both standing up and lying down.

before: This British neurologist's case study of patients who had been immobilized for decades by the 1920s encephalitis lethargica epidemic inspired both a 1973 memoir and a 1990 film starring Robin Williams and Robert De Niro; The New York Times called him a 'poet laureate of contemporary medicine.'
after: This British neurologist's case study of patients who had been immobilized for decades by the 1920s encephalitis lethargica epidemic inspired both a 1973 memoir and a 1990 film starring Robin Williams and Robert De Niro.

before: Derived from the Latin for 'whip,' these hair-like appendages provide motility to a wide range of microorganisms; Helicobacter pylori uses them to propel itself through the stomach toward the mucous lining, where it can cause gastritis and ulcers.
after: Derived from the Latin for 'whip,' these hair-like appendages provide motility to a wide range of microorganisms; some bacteria use them to propel themselves through the stomach toward the mucous lining, where they can cause gastritis and ulcers.

before: This viral hemorrhagic fever, first reported in sub-Saharan Africa in 1976, spreads through direct contact with body fluids; outbreaks have had mortality rates ranging from 25 to 90 percent, averaging around 50 percent, with fruit bats believed to be the natural host.
after: This viral hemorrhagic fever, first reported in sub-Saharan Africa in 1976, spreads through direct contact with body fluids; outbreaks have had mortality rates ranging from 25 to 90 percent, with fruit bats believed to be the natural host.

before: Affecting roughly 1 in 10 adults globally between ages 30 and 69, this breathing disorder causes repetitive overnight pauses; its most common form results from collapse of the upper airway and is often treated with a device that maintains continuous positive airway pressure.
after: Affecting roughly 1 in 10 adults globally between ages 30 and 69, this disorder causes breathing to repeatedly stop and restart through the night; its most common form results from collapse of the upper airway and is often treated with a device that maintains continuous positive airway pressure.

before: In 1901, this German physicist became the inaugural Nobel laureate in Physics for discovering electromagnetic radiation in a wavelength range whose nature was initially unknown; atomic number 111 on the periodic table was later named in his honor.
after: In 1901, this German physicist became the inaugural Nobel laureate in Physics for discovering a new, penetrating form of electromagnetic radiation whose nature was at first a mystery; atomic number 111 on the periodic table was later named in his honor.

before: The first probe to achieve interplanetary spaceflight flew past this planet in 1961; the first spacecraft to land on its surface arrived in 1970; a 1974 mission captured the first photographs of it during a gravity assist en route to another planet.
after: The first probe to achieve interplanetary spaceflight flew past this planet in 1961; the first spacecraft to land on its surface arrived in 1970.

before: This small, multi-toed North American mammal of roughly 45 to 55 million years ago was the evolutionary predecessor of today's large, single-hoofed grazers; the domesticated descendant it eventually became was first tamed in Central Asia around 4,000 BCE.
after: Long classified under the genus Hyracotherium, this extinct genus of small, multi-toed equids from the Early Eocene — known only from fossils unearthed in North America — is the earliest well-known ancestor of the modern horse.

before: Members of the class Diplopoda, these arthropods are named from the Latin for 'thousand feet' — though they actually have far fewer; each of their elongated body segments is formed from two fused single segments, giving them their characteristic paired-legs appearance.
after: These many-legged arthropods roll into a tight spiral when threatened and move with a slow, rippling wave of their legs; each of their body rings is formed from two fused segments, giving them two pairs of legs per ring.

before: The temperature to which air must be cooled — at constant pressure — for its relative humidity to reach 100 percent, at which point water vapor begins to condense into liquid droplets.
after: The temperature at which air, cooled at constant pressure, becomes saturated and its water vapor begins to condense into liquid droplets.

before: Its chemical symbol Au derives from the Latin aurum; this dense, malleable transition metal with atomic number 79 is the second-least reactive of all elements, surpassed in that regard only by platinum.
after: This dense, malleable transition metal with atomic number 79 is the second-least reactive of all elements, surpassed in that regard only by platinum.

before: In particle physics, this elementary particle of half-integer spin does not undergo strong interactions and comes in six types — including the electron, muon, and tauon among the charged class, plus three corresponding neutrinos.
after: This elementary particle of half-integer spin does not undergo strong interactions and comes in six types — including the electron, muon, and tauon among the charged class, plus three corresponding neutrinos.

before: Caused by the protozoan Trypanosoma brucei, which is transmitted through the bite of an infected tsetse fly, this insect-borne African disease is formally known as human African trypanosomiasis.
after: Caused by the protozoan Trypanosoma brucei and transmitted through the bite of an infected tsetse fly, this parasitic disease endemic to sub-Saharan Africa causes fever, swollen lymph nodes, and eventual neurological deterioration if untreated.

before: Each year, up to 450,000 of the lesser subspecies of this large North American bird (Antigone canadensis) converge on the central Platte River Valley in Nebraska during their annual migration — the most important stopover for the species.
after: Each year, up to 450,000 members of the lesser subspecies of this large, red-capped North American bird converge on the central Platte River Valley in Nebraska — the most critical stopover in their annual migration from Gulf Coast wintering grounds to Arctic breeding territory.

before: When a bar of this element with atomic number 50 is slowly bent by hand, its crystals undergo a process called twinning that emits a characteristic crackling sound — a phenomenon that has lent the element's own name to the noise it makes.
after: When a bar of this atomic-number-50 element is slowly bent by hand, its crystal lattice undergoes twinning — a deformation process that produces a distinctive crackling sound audible without any instruments.

before: Unlike the other three major golf championships, which rotate among different courses, this annual April tournament is always held at Augusta National Golf Club in Georgia.
after: Unlike the other three major golf championships, which rotate among different courses, this one is played every April at the same venue: Augusta National Golf Club in Georgia.

before: This American Grade I stakes race run at Churchill Downs in Louisville is contested by three-year-old Thoroughbreds at a distance of 1+1⁄4 miles; colts and geldings carry 126 pounds while fillies carry 121.
after: This American Grade I stakes race run at Churchill Downs in Louisville is contested by three-year-old Thoroughbreds at a distance of 1+1⁄4 miles.

before: First held in 1897 and inspired by the inaugural Olympic road race of 1896, this annual event through greater Massachusetts is the world's oldest annual road race of its distance.
after: Inspired by the inaugural Olympic road race of 1896 and first held the very next year, this is the world's oldest annual race of its distance, traditionally run each Patriots' Day.

before: A long-distance foot race usually run as a road race, this event spans 42.195 kilometres but can also be covered on trail routes; more than 800 are held worldwide each year.
after: Coined by the French philologist Michel Bréal for the inaugural modern Olympics of 1896 in Athens, this race drew its name from an Ancient Greek story of a runner rushing to deliver news of battle.

before: This two-player strategy game of Chinese origin, in which players place stones to fence off territory on a grid, is believed to be the oldest board game continuously played to the present day.
after: Played with black and white stones on a 19-by-19 grid, this ancient Chinese game has more legal board positions than there are atoms in the observable universe.

before: This Olympic combat sport, conducted in a ring with padded gloves, pits two opponents against each other for a predetermined number of rounds, with the winner decided by points or a stoppage.
after: This combat sport, conducted in a ring with padded gloves, pits two opponents against each other for a predetermined number of rounds, with the winner decided by points or a stoppage.

before: First organized in 1903 to boost newspaper sales, this annual multi-stage cycling race is the oldest and most prestigious of road cycling's three Grand Tours.
after: First organized in 1903 to boost sales of the newspaper L'Auto, this roughly three-week race awards a polka-dot jersey to its best climber and a green jersey to its top sprinter.

before: In this casino game, a croupier sends a ball spinning inside a bowl against a rotating numbered wheel; players bet on which pocket the ball will ultimately settle in, wagering on color, number, or grouping.
after: Deriving from a French word for one who rides behind — a nod to the reserve of cash once kept at a gambler's back — this casino employee deals the cards, spins the wheel, and rakes in the losing chips at the gaming table.

before: Two teams of eleven players use curved sticks to propel a small, hard ball into the opposing goal in this fast-paced team sport, which is played on artificial turf and is simply called by its non-qualifier name across Asia, Oceania, and parts of Europe.
after: Two teams of eleven use curved sticks to drive a small, hard ball into the opposing goal in this fast-paced sport played on artificial turf, an Olympic staple often confused with its ice-bound cousin.

before: This AL East franchise, which has called Fenway Park home since 1912, endured an 86-year World Series drought — attributed to the sale of a star player to a bitter rival — before finally winning the championship in 2004.
after: This AL East franchise endured an 86-year World Series drought — popularly blamed on the sale of a star player to a bitter rival — before finally winning the championship in 2004.

before: Contested over two days at major track-and-field competitions, this women's combined event encompasses seven disciplines and has been a regular Olympic program entry since the 1984 Los Angeles Games.
after: Contested over two days, this women's combined track-and-field event — the counterpart to the men's ten-event contest — has been on the Olympic program since the 1984 Los Angeles Games.

before: Composed of 32 teams across the United States and Canada, this professional league awards the Stanley Cup — the oldest professional sports trophy in North America — to its annual playoff champion.
after: Composed of 32 teams across the United States and Canada, this professional circuit awards the Stanley Cup — the oldest professional sports trophy in North America — to its annual playoff champion.

before: After leading Michigan State to the 1979 NCAA championship, this player was selected first overall by the Los Angeles Lakers and went on to be widely regarded as the greatest point guard the NBA has ever seen.
after: As a rookie, this player started at center in place of an injured Kareem Abdul-Jabbar for the deciding game of the 1980 NBA Finals, posting 42 points and 15 rebounds to seize the series MVP.

before: Turning professional in 1996 at age 20, this golfer won his first major the following April by a record 12 strokes and ultimately tied the all-time PGA Tour wins record while standing second only to Jack Nicklaus in career major championships.
after: Legally named Eldrick, this golfer is universally known by a nickname his father gave him in honor of a South Vietnamese army comrade.

before: This forward won three championships and four MVP awards in the American Basketball Association, helping legitimize the rival league as its biggest star before the ABA merged with the NBA in 1976.
after: In Game 4 of the 1980 NBA Finals, this high-flying forward drove the right baseline, floated behind the backboard, and reverse-scooped the ball in for a layup so improbable it is still called simply 'the Move' — four years after he had soared from the foul line to win basketball's first slam dunk contest.

before: He surpassed Pete Sampras's record of 14 men's Grand Slam titles at Wimbledon in 2009 and ultimately won a record eight championships at that tournament before retiring in September 2022.
after: It took this player roughly five years on tour to capture his first major title, but he went on to hold the world No. 1 ranking for a record 237 consecutive weeks.

before: Designed by Scottish architect Alister MacKenzie for the retired amateur champion Bobby Jones and opened in 1932, this private course in Georgia has hosted an invitational major every April since 1934.
after: Designed by Scottish architect Alister MacKenzie for the retired amateur champion Bobby Jones and opened in 1932, this exclusive course is renowned for the perilous run of holes along Rae's Creek that sportswriters christened 'Amen Corner.'

before: Its name is French for 'little wheel,' and it likely evolved from the Italian game Biribi; in this casino game, players bet on a numbered pocket where a spinning ball will come to rest.
after: This casino game is named from the French for 'little wheel'; it likely evolved from the Italian game Biribi.

before: In this tabletop competition — known as 'table football' in Europe — players score by rotating rods fitted with tiny figures, sending a small sphere into the opponent's goal.
after: In this game, players spin rows of rod-mounted figures to drive a small ball into the opponent's goal.

before: Along with the cross, one of two punches classified as a power punch in boxing statistics, this strike is delivered from a low position and travels vertically toward an opponent's chin.
after: Thrown upward from a low position toward an opponent's chin, this boxing punch is counted — along with the cross — as one of the two 'power punches' in the sport's statistics.

before: Coined during the 1988–89 Los Angeles Lakers season — when the two-time defending champions sought another consecutive title — this portmanteau for a run of successive wins entered the sports lexicon.
after: This term entered the sports lexicon during the Los Angeles Lakers' 1988–89 season, when the back-to-back defending champions fell short in their bid for another championship.

before: The only individual in NBA history to be honored as Rookie of the Year, Most Valuable Player, Finals MVP, All-Star MVP, Coach of the Year, and Executive of the Year.
after: He is the only person in NBA history to be named Rookie of the Year, Most Valuable Player, Finals MVP, All-Star Game MVP, Coach of the Year, and Executive of the Year.

before: Name this painting.
after: Name the artist.

before: Name this painting.
after: Name the artist.

before: Name this painting.
after: Name the artist.

before: Name this painting.
after: Name the artist.

before: This Latin aphorism, translated as 'seize the day,' appears in book 1 of the Roman poet Horace's Odes, composed around 23 BC.
after: This Latin aphorism, translated as 'seize the day,' first appears in book 1 of the Roman poet Horace's Odes, composed around 23 BC.

before: This English word for a food of filling placed between two slices of bread takes its name from John Montagu, an 18th-century British nobleman so devoted to gaming that he reputedly ate his meal without leaving the card table.
after: This English word for a filling served between two slices of bread takes its name from John Montagu, an 18th-century British nobleman so devoted to gaming that he reputedly ate his meals without leaving the card table.

before: The substitution of an inoffensive or mild expression for one that might be considered unpleasant — often used when discussing death, disability, sex, or violence — defines this common rhetorical device.
after: This common rhetorical device swaps a blunt or unpleasant term for a gentler one, as when death, disability, or violence is referred to only indirectly.

before: This German loanword, literally translating as 'harm-joy,' names the pleasure derived from learning of another's troubles, failures, or humiliation.
after: Literally translating as 'harm-joy,' this German loanword names the pleasure derived from learning of another's troubles, failures, or humiliation.

before: In folklore and fiction, this German-origin loanword names a supernatural twin of a living person, perceived as a harbinger of bad luck.
after: This German-derived term names a ghostly twin of a living person — to meet one's own, folklore warns, foretells bad luck.

before: French for 'already seen,' this phrase names the eerie phenomenon of feeling that a present moment has been experienced before — an illusion of memory with no verifiable prior context.
after: The uncanny conviction that you have lived this exact moment before — an illusion of memory rather than true recollection — goes by this French phrase for 'already seen.'

before: This English word for organized non-participation entered the language in 1880, named after a County Mayo land agent in Ireland who was subjected to the tactic at the suggestion of nationalist leader Charles Stewart Parnell.
after: Coined in 1880, this word for organized abstention honors a County Mayo land agent in Ireland who was shunned by the community at the urging of nationalist leader Charles Stewart Parnell.

before: Words like 'oink,' 'meow,' 'roar,' and 'chirp' — as well as 'beep,' 'simmer,' and 'hiccup' — are all examples of this rhetorical term for a word that phonetically imitates the sound it represents.
after: Words like 'oink,' 'meow,' and 'chirp' are examples of this rhetorical term for a word that phonetically imitates the sound it represents.

before: Originally the name of a Romance-based pidgin used by traders across the Mediterranean Basin from the 11th to the 19th centuries, this Italian-origin phrase now refers to any language used as a common medium between groups with different native tongues.
after: Originally the name of a Romance-based trade language used by merchants across the Mediterranean Basin from the 11th to the 19th centuries, this Italian-origin phrase now denotes any tongue adopted as a common medium between groups with different native languages.

before: Lewis Carroll coined this linguistic term in Through the Looking-Glass (1871) to describe a word blended from two others — as in 'smog' from 'smoke' and 'fog,' or 'motel' from 'motor' and 'hotel.'
after: Lewis Carroll coined this linguistic term in Through the Looking-Glass (1871) to describe a word blended from the sounds and meanings of two others.

before: This everyday English word for a building of exhibits and collections descends, by way of the Greek mouseion, from a shrine to the nine goddesses of poetry, music, and the arts.
after: This everyday English word for a building of exhibits and collections descends from a group of nine Greek goddesses of poetry, music, and the arts.

before: This public health measure restricts movement of people or animals potentially exposed to a communicable disease — distinct from isolation, which applies only to confirmed cases; the Yorkshire village of Eyam famously adopted it voluntarily in 1665 to contain the bubonic plague.
after: Named from the Italian for the time arriving ships were held offshore in Venetian and Ragusan ports during the 14th-century Black Death, this measure isolates people or animals possibly exposed to a contagious disease.

before: Named for the Warden of New College, Oxford who reportedly committed it often, this speech phenomenon involves accidentally swapping initial consonant sounds between two words — producing, for example, "runny babbit" from "bunny rabbit."
after: Named for a Warden of New College, Oxford who was prone to the slip, this is the accidental swap of the initial sounds of two words — for example turning 'bunny rabbit' into 'runny babbit.'

before: This Latin phrase for the existing state of affairs often appears in policy debates when current conditions are being measured against proposed change; its extended form, with the word "ante" appended, means conditions as they stood before a particular event.
after: This Latin phrase for the existing state of affairs often surfaces in policy debates when present conditions are weighed against proposed change.

before: In law and common usage alike, this Latin phrase denotes sincere, honest intention — the direct opposite of its counterpart "mala fides," which means bad faith or duplicity.
after: In law and everyday usage alike, this Latin phrase denotes sincere, honest intention — acting in good faith, with no attempt to deceive.

before: Borrowed into Australian English from Wiradjuri, an Aboriginal language of New South Wales, this term names a small isolated body of water — such as an oxbow lake or river backwater formed when a watercourse changes its course.
after: Borrowed into Australian English from Wiradjuri, an Aboriginal language of New South Wales, this term names a small isolated body of water, such as an oxbow lake or river backwater, formed when a watercourse changes its course.

before: Classified as a parasomnia disorder of state dissociation, this condition occurs during the slow-wave N3 stage of non-REM sleep when features of deep slumber and wakefulness arise simultaneously in different brain regions — episodes typically last from thirty seconds to thirty minutes.
after: Classified as a parasomnia of state dissociation, this condition occurs when features of deep slumber and wakefulness surface at the same time and a person carries out complex actions with no memory of them.

before: The son of Cush and great-grandson of Noah in Genesis, this king of Shinar is described as "a mighty hunter before the Lord" and is identified by Jewish, Christian, and Islamic tradition as the ruler who led the builders of the Tower of Babel.
after: Originally the name of a hunter of renown in Genesis, this term morphed into slang for an inept or foolish person after 1940s cartoons in which Bugs Bunny sarcastically used it to call Elmer Fudd a great hunter.

before: In linguistics, this term describes any newly formed word, term, or phrase that has gained sufficient popular or institutional recognition to be entering mainstream language — 'selfie' was once one.
after: This linguistic term describes a word taken from a person's name — such as 'sandwich,' 'cardigan,' or the unit 'watt.'

before: Named after an Oxford don who reportedly told students they had 'hissed all his mystery lectures' and produced examples like 'blushing crow' for 'crushing blow,' this term describes the accidental transposition of initial consonants between words.
after: This type of sentence contains every letter of the alphabet at least once — most famously 'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.'

before: This clinical term for a parasomnia describes a state of dissociation in which features of wakefulness and deep non-REM sleep occur simultaneously in different brain regions, causing a person to perform normal activities while technically asleep.
after: Borrowed directly from French, this word names a listless weariness and dissatisfaction that arises from boredom or a lack of excitement.

before: The English village of Eyam famously imposed this public health measure on itself in 1665, voluntarily sealing its borders so the bubonic plague it was suffering from could not spread to neighboring communities.
after: The surname of an 18th-century German physician who claimed to heal patients through 'animal magnetism' is the origin of this word, meaning 'to hold someone so spellbound that they seem entranced.'

before: This execution apparatus was designed during the Enlightenment to make capital punishment more reliable and humane; its very name dates from the French Revolution, though similar devices had existed in Europe for centuries before then, and France last used it on September 10, 1977.
after: This close-fitting one-piece garment worn by dancers and gymnasts takes its name from the 19th-century French acrobat who popularized the flying trapeze.

before: Cutting portrait profiles from black card became popular in the mid-18th century, and this term for such images — now also applied to any dark shape seen against a lighter background — entered common English use in the early 19th century.
after: These patches of facial hair grown down the cheeks owe their name — with its two parts swapped — to a prominently whiskered Union general of the American Civil War.

before: In Australian English, this term for a small body of still water — such as an oxbow lake or a river channel that leads to a dead end — is likely borrowed from Wiradjuri, an Aboriginal Australian language of New South Wales.
after: Also known as a Janus word, this is a term for a word that functions as its own opposite — 'cleave,' for example, can mean both to split apart and to cling together.

before: This Latin phrase, literally meaning 'in good faith,' describes a sincere intention to be fair and honest regardless of outcome, and is an important concept in both contract law and business negotiations.
after: Literally 'world-pain' in German, this term names a weary melancholy felt at the gap between the world as it is and the world as one wishes it could be.

before: This Latin phrase for the existing state of affairs is commonly used in policy debate to contrast current conditions with a proposed change, as in 'maintaining the nuclear arsenals at current levels.'
after: Coined by the writer Sylvia Wright in 1954, this term for a misheard word or phrase — most often a misinterpreted song lyric — arose when she reinterpreted a ballad's line 'laid him on the green' as the name of a slain noblewoman.

before: This French phrase — literally 'already seen' — names the uncanny psychological sensation of experiencing a seemingly new situation as deeply familiar, as if one has lived through it before.
after: This rhetorical term names the insertion of a word into the middle of another word or phrase, as in 'abso-bloody-lutely' or 'a whole nother.'

before: Patented in Paris in 1846 by the Belgian inventor whose surname it carries, this single-reed woodwind is found in both symphony orchestras and jazz ensembles.
after: The name for this instrument was coined from the surname of the Belgian instrument-maker who patented his invention in Paris in 1846 — a rare case of a major orchestral and jazz instrument named for the single person who built it.

before: This verb for illegally entering a building to steal is a back-formation: the older form of the word is the noun for the person who commits the act, and the verb was derived by stripping that noun's suffix.
after: This 16th-century word for someone who breaks into buildings to steal traces back through Medieval Latin to the Latin word for a fortified town or fortress; curiously, its otherwise-unexplained 'l' may have been inserted under the influence of the Latin 'latro,' meaning thief.

before: This figure of speech is at work when 'suits' stands for business executives or 'wheels' stands for a car, using a part to represent the whole or vice versa.
after: This figure of speech uses a part to stand for the whole, or the whole for a part — as when 'suits' means executives or 'wheels' means a car.

before: Kennedy's famous inaugural line 'Ask not what your country can do for you; ask what you can do for your country' exemplifies this rhetorical device, in which successive clauses reverse each other's grammatical structure.
after: Kennedy's famous inaugural line 'Ask not what your country can do for you; ask what you can do for your country' exemplifies this rhetorical device in which successive clauses reverse each other's grammatical structure.

before: In the vocabulary of heraldry, this Old French and Latin term names the paler of the two metals permitted on a coat of arms, serving as the counterpart to 'or,' which represents gold.
after: In the vocabulary of heraldry, this Old French and Latin term names the paler of the two precious metals permitted on a coat of arms.

before: From the Latin 'frater,' meaning 'brother,' this verb describes establishing relations that violate social, professional, or military norms — often used of soldiers who associate with those on the opposing side.
after: This verb describes forming relationships that breach social, professional, or military norms — most often used of soldiers who get friendly with those on the opposing side.

before: A German philosopher used this adjective — borrowed from an astronomer's surname — to describe his own proposed revolution in epistemology, arguing that objects of experience conform to the mind's forms rather than the other way around.
after: Immanuel Kant used this adjective — borrowed from an astronomer's surname — to describe his own proposed revolution in epistemology, arguing that objects of experience conform to the mind's forms rather than the other way around.

before: Born in British political vocabulary during the Second Boer War, this phrase names a parliamentary contest deliberately scheduled at a moment of wartime patriotic fervor — the term derives from the color of the British soldier's battlefield uniform.
after: Born in political vocabulary during the Second Boer War, this phrase, stemming from the color of British soldiers' battlefield uniforms, names a parliamentary contest deliberately scheduled at a moment of wartime patriotic fervor.
